question: A man in his 50s with hepatitis C cirrhosis presented with progressive right upper-quadrant pain. Cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a solitary hepatic mass and percutaneous biopsy confirmed a malignant neoplasm that did not respond to antibiotics. Based on the imaging, which surgical procedure is most appropriate?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the CT image for lesion location and characteristics. The scan shows a solitary, large heterogeneous mass occupying most of the right hepatic lobe. The lesion has central low attenuation and more peripheral higher-density tissue, producing an overall necrotic/heterogeneous appearance; the left lobe appears separate and free of a similar mass.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical stem: the patient has hepatitis C cirrhosis, progressive RUQ pain, a percutaneous biopsy that confirmed a malignant neoplasm, and no response to antibiotics. These facts indicate the lesion is a cancerous hepatic mass rather than an infected abscess that would resolve with antibiotics or simple drainage.

Then, I consider how each procedural option aligns with the image and clinical data. A left hepatectomy would not remove a lesion centered in the right lobe, so option B is anatomically mismatched. Percutaneous drainage and culture (option C) is directed at an abscess, but the biopsy is malignant and antibiotics failed, so drainage does not address the underlying cancer. Radiofrequency ablation (option D) is less appropriate for a large, heterogeneous/necrotic mass occupying most of the right lobe because complete local tumor control would be difficult from the imaging appearance.

Finally, tying the observations together: because the mass is solitary, large, and located within the right hepatic lobe, and it is confirmed malignant, surgical resection of the right lobe (right hepatectomy) best addresses the lesion visible on imaging.
caption: CT showing 4.8 cm × 4.2 cm × 5.9 cm peripheral low-attenuation lesion with small peripheral septations within the right hepatic lobe.
sub_caption: CT showing 4.8 cm × 4.2 cm × 5.9 cm peripheral low-attenuation lesion with small peripheral septations within the right hepatic lobe.
image_context: A man in his 50s with a history of hepatitis C virus and liver cirrhosis presented with 5 days of progressive right upper quadrant pain. A computed tomography scan (CT scan) of the abdomen and pelvis showed a large liver mass (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="ccrep-9-2016-031f1">Fig. 1</xref>). The patient was treated for possible liver abscess with antibiotics. The patient did not improve with antibiotics. A magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was done due to persistence of symptoms, which demonstrated a 4.4 cm × 4.8 cm × 4.8 cm right hepatic lobe mass with a large area of necrosis with peripheral enhancement (). The patient was treated for possible liver abscess with antibiotics. The patient did not improve with antibiotics. A magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was done due to persistence of symptoms, which demonstrated a 4.4 cm × 4.8 cm × 4.8 cm right hepatic lobe mass with a large area of necrosis with peripheral enhancement (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="ccrep-9-2016-031f2">Fig. 2</xref>). Subsequently, a liver biopsy was performed that demonstrated a hepatic osteosarcoma. The core biopsy yielded fibroblastic malignant cells producing lace-like osteoid matrix (). Subsequently, a liver biopsy was performed that demonstrated a hepatic osteosarcoma. The core biopsy yielded fibroblastic malignant cells producing lace-like osteoid matrix (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="ccrep-9-2016-031f3">Fig. 3</xref>). Rare osteoclast-like giant cells and mitotic figures were also seen (). Rare osteoclast-like giant cells and mitotic figures were also seen (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="ccrep-9-2016-031f4">Fig. 4</xref>). Liver parenchyma and carcinomatous element were absent. Extensive sampling of the liver did not show any evidence of carcinomatous or sarcomatous transformation, ruling out choriocarcinoma. Core biopsies of both benign and malignant liver tissues did not show any embryonic or fetal pattern differentiation, thereby excluding hepatoblastoma and hepatocellular carcinoma. On immunohistochemistry, the tumor cells were negative for HepPar1 and AE1/3 and reactive for vimentin. A whole-body positron emission tomography–computed tomography (PET/CT) scan also demonstrated malignant-level uptake in the 1.8 cm × 0.9 cm soft tissue nodule just anterior to the patellar surface of the left distal femur, later confirmed by MRI. Excisional biopsy of the deep left knee mass, which revealed pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS) and histological feature, did not resemble the tumor in the liver. The diagnosis of primary osteosarcoma was made for which the patient underwent portal vein embolization in preparation for a surgical resection of the right liver lobe. He was admitted six weeks after the embolization for dyspnea and abdominal distension and expired two weeks later.). Liver parenchyma and carcinomatous element were absent. Extensive sampling of the liver did not show any evidence of carcinomatous or sarcomatous transformation, ruling out choriocarcinoma. Core biopsies of both benign and malignant liver tissues did not show any embryonic or fetal pattern differentiation, thereby excluding hepatoblastoma and hepatocellular carcinoma. On immunohistochemistry, the tumor cells were negative for HepPar1 and AE1/3 and reactive for vimentin. A whole-body positron emission tomography–computed tomography (PET/CT) scan also demonstrated malignant-level uptake in the 1.8 cm × 0.9 cm soft tissue nodule just anterior to the patellar surface of the left distal femur, later confirmed by MRI. Excisional biopsy of the deep left knee mass, which revealed pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS) and histological feature, did not resemble the tumor in the liver. The diagnosis of primary osteosarcoma was made for which the patient underwent portal vein embolization in preparation for a surgical resection of the right liver lobe. He was admitted six weeks after the embolization for dyspnea and abdominal distension and expired two weeks later.
